key_insight: The paper introduces a fatigue damage model where degradation accumulates based on distance to an endurance surface, enabling physically meaningful endurance limits and damage growth over many cycles.
engineering_relevance: It provides a thermodynamically consistent, mesh-objective framework for predicting high-cycle fatigue and anisotropic damage in quasi-brittle and metallic materials under complex loading.
method: Uses the energy-release rate as the thermodynamic driving force for damage evolution.
Defines damage growth by the distance of the driving force from an endurance surface, rather than a classic failure surface.
Adds micromorphic gradient regularization plus anisotropic damage and crack-closure/reopening effects for multiaxial, path-dependent fatigue.
evidence_signal: The model is calibrated against monotonic plain-concrete behavior and high-cycle fatigue data for low-alloy steel, then tested on an L-shaped concrete specimen, stress-life loading, and combined axial-torsional fatigue.
watch_item: The framework adds constitutive and gradient-enhancement complexity, so calibration and computational cost may be nontrivial for practical simulations.
paper_title: Modeling Fatigue-Induced Anisotropic Quasi-Brittle Damage Based on the Endurance Surface Concept
